event_sheets.title: Warm Badges
event_sheets.prompt: A dispenser spits out fresh employee badges, all still warm. None of the names match the people in your party. The smiling screen above it thanks you for participating in accelerated career visibility.

choices.label: Sort through them carefully
choices.summary: Play it safe. Look for a useful mismatch.

choices.label: Jam the machine
choices.summary: Let Facilities Goblin break the system before it tags you.

result_short.take-highest-rank-badge: Gain immediate leverage, but Meridian notices the discrepancy.
result_short.sort-through-carefully: Gain steadier value with less scrutiny.
result_short.jam-the-machine: Prevent the system from assigning you cleanly and pull something useful out of the wreckage.

result_long.take-highest-rank-badge: You choose the badge with the highest title because Meridian still believes rank should open doors faster than truth. The scanner accepts you just long enough to be dangerous. Somewhere deeper in the building, something updates your file with interest. This is the kind of access Crown Meridian never meant to give you, which is why it arrives trailing suspicion.
result_long.sort-through-carefully: You ignore the flattering titles and look for the least-wrong lie. The dispenser keeps spitting warm plastic while you search, as if the building is impatient for you to become someone easier to route. You leave with a badge that does not fit, but fits well enough to survive on.
result_long.jam-the-machine: Facilities Goblin gets fingers, wire, and delighted malice into the housing before the system can decide who you are. The dispenser shrieks, swallows three names, and coughs up a half-valid badge core and a shower of static. Meridian hates damaged paperwork because damaged paperwork can still be argued with.

companion_bark.speaker: Facilities Goblin
companion_bark.line: Good. If it starts smoking, it’s finally being honest.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: Titles are only useful if you know which doors they offend.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: Meridian has always trusted badges more than people.

event_sheets.title: Applause Threshold
event_sheets.prompt: A sealed door will not open until the room registers enough applause. The overhead speakers keep insisting that enthusiasm is voluntary. Dark streaks mark the floor where people stood too long deciding whether to clap.

choices.label: Clap along
choices.summary: Easy progress. Keep your head down.

choices.label: Refuse and wait
choices.summary: Test whether the system blinks first.

result_short.clap-along: The door opens. You hate how easy that was.
result_short.spoof-the-sensor: You fake approval and learn how shallow the system’s standards are.
result_short.refuse-and-wait: The room escalates before it yields, but it does yield. Barely.

result_long.clap-along: You clap. The speakers brighten. The applause track fattens around your own until the room can pretend it was earned. The door opens with the smug grace of a policy being proven right, and you walk through feeling slightly less like a person and slightly more like usable data.
result_long.spoof-the-sensor: You feed the sensor what it wants without giving it the satisfaction of sincerity. The room accepts the fraud immediately, which tells you something useful about Crown Meridian: even its rituals were built to detect compliance, not belief. The door slides open to pre-recorded encouragement that no one present deserves.
result_long.refuse-and-wait: You stand still while the room cycles through incentives, reminders, and gentle threats. The speakers become sharper. The lights become hotter. At the point where a kinder building would have given up, Meridian finally relents, not because it respects refusal, but because it has finished documenting it.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: Anything that calls coercion voluntary is already on company letterhead.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: If they needed applause this badly, the pitch was never good.

companion_bark.speaker: Facilities Goblin
companion_bark.line: Can I clap with a wrench? It feels more sincere.

event_sheets.title: Career Accelerator
event_sheets.prompt: A spinning prize wheel promises a career accelerator package if you sign before reading the deduction schedule. Every wedge on the wheel is gold except the tiny ones that look legally important.

result_short.spin-now: Strong upside arrives attached to hidden debt.
result_short.read-the-deductions: You keep the reward modest and the damage legible.
result_short.forge-a-signature: You steal the benefit and let the paper trail rot elsewhere.

result_long.spin-now: The wheel spins with the energy of a game show trying not to become a confession. It lands on something generous enough to feel suspicious, and the contract seals before the applause dies. Later, when the cost shows up in fine print and blood pressure, you understand why Meridian loves acceleration: speed is just a way to outrun informed consent.
result_long.read-the-deductions: You ignore the lights and read. The wheel keeps trying to hurry you, which tells you the danger is in the paperwork, not the prize. What you claim is smaller, cleaner, and much harder for the building to leverage against you. In Meridian Spire, restraint is one of the few luxuries that still compounds.
result_long.forge-a-signature: You give the form a name Meridian deserves more than you do. The system accepts it because authority has always mattered more here than authenticity. The payout arrives hot and immediate. Somewhere down the line, someone else will get called into a meeting about it, which is how Crown Meridian has always preferred its consequences distributed.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: Never sign anything that smiles before you do.

companion_bark.speaker: Payroll Wraith
companion_bark.line: Deferred cost is still cost. Meridian simply charges interest in suffering.

companion_bark.speaker: Facilities Goblin
companion_bark.line: Spin it. Worst case, we learn something expensive.

event_sheets.title: Printer Choir
event_sheets.prompt: A row of office printers hums in harmony, feeding out incident reports for things that have not happened yet. The toner smells hot, prophetic, and slightly vindictive.

result_short.read-your-own-report: You gain foresight and lose some peace.
result_short.shred-the-stack: You prevent one version of the future at the cost of useful warning.
result_short.swap-the-top-page: You change who the paperwork thinks is at fault. That never stays harmless for long.

result_long.read-your-own-report: The page already knows your name, your status, and the tone Meridian will use after you survive or fail. Reading it gives you an edge, but it also reveals how early the building starts preparing its explanations. The most upsetting part is not that the report predicts harm. It is that the report is written like the harm already received approval.
result_long.shred-the-stack: You feed future incident reports into the shredder until the room sounds like paper being denied its afterlife. The printers object in perfect harmony. For a moment, the floor feels less inevitable. Then the red warning lights start to pulse, and you remember that destroying a narrative is not the same thing as escaping it.
result_long.swap-the-top-page: You change the order, not the appetite. Someone else’s future report now sits where yours used to be, and the system accepts the edit with bureaucratic indifference. It is a dark little revelation: Crown Meridian never cared who suffered, only that the paperwork stayed continuous.

companion_bark.speaker: Payroll Wraith
companion_bark.line: Predicted loss is still entered as acceptable variance. Of course it is.

companion_bark.speaker: Compliance
companion_bark.line: A report written early is still evidence.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: If the paperwork arrives first, the emergency is already owned by management.

event_sheets.title: Missed Lunch
event_sheets.prompt: The break room clock says lunch ended three years ago. A vending machine offers one free item if you can prove you were never paid for the last one.

result_short.make-the-case: You win something small and morally overdue.
result_short.shake-the-machine: You get value quickly, but the machine remembers the insult.
result_short.walk-away: You lose the payout and keep your momentum.

result_long.make-the-case: You explain, with more accuracy than dignity, how Meridian has been billing your exhaustion back to you for years. The vending machine clicks, thinks, and finally dispenses a reward like it hates being compelled by fairness. Payroll Wraith watches the whole thing with the particular stillness of someone who has seen too many debts dressed as procedure.
result_long.shake-the-machine: You stop negotiating with a device whose business model is petty theft and give it the kind of clarity only violence provides. The snack coil snaps, the casing dents, and several items tumble free in a victory that feels crude but understandable. Somewhere behind the metal, a service log quietly updates your temperament.
result_long.walk-away: You leave. The clock keeps lying. The machine keeps offering a reward that is really an argument about what your time is worth. There is no triumph in refusing it, but there is a certain economy in not spending one more minute proving you deserved lunch in the first place.

companion_bark.speaker: Payroll Wraith
companion_bark.line: Unpaid time compounds. Meridian merely prefers not to label the interest.

companion_bark.speaker: Facilities Goblin
companion_bark.line: You can appeal to the machine or we can make it educational.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: The company never forgets a claim. It only postpones admitting it.

event_sheets.title: Performance Audit
event_sheets.prompt: A glass meeting room lights up with your metrics. Some of them are impossible. All of them are somehow your responsibility.

choices.label: Accept and exploit
choices.summary: Take the hit, steal the leverage.

choices.label: Cut the power
choices.summary: End the meeting the honest way.

result_short.defend-yourself: You keep your footing, but Meridian records the resistance.
result_short.accept-and-exploit: You let the review land, then weaponize the paperwork it creates.
result_short.cut-the-power: You break the frame instead of arguing inside it.

result_long.defend-yourself: You answer each metric like it was composed by someone too senior to admit arithmetic error. The room listens with legal patience. You do not convince the system, but you do force it to become more specific, which is one of the few ways to injure bureaucracy without touching it.
result_long.accept-and-exploit: You sit through the review with the composure of someone stealing from the meeting in real time. By the end, the room has generated enough official language to create an opening somewhere else. This is the kind of Crown Meridian skill Former Executive Assistant learned young: let them write the trap, then take the key from the footer.
result_long.cut-the-power: The presentation dies mid-accusation. The room falls silent except for the building’s irritated ventilation and Facilities Goblin’s obvious delight. Whatever else happens next, at least the meeting has briefly stopped pretending it was about performance. It was always about obedience wearing charts.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: If the numbers are impossible, the desired confession is not numerical.

companion_bark.speaker: Facilities Goblin
companion_bark.line: I vote we audit the wiring.

companion_bark.speaker: Human Resources
companion_bark.line: This room confuses evaluation with containment.

event_sheets.title: Wellness Check-In
event_sheets.prompt: A mandatory wellness kiosk asks whether you feel supported. The “no” button is grayed out, but not completely. A soft voice thanks you for your honesty before you answer.

result_short.press-yes: You receive comfort with strings attached.
result_short.force-no: You earn a harder truth and a sharper kind of help.
result_short.answer-for-someone-else: You misdirect the system and complicate the paper trail.

result_long.press-yes: The kiosk rewards your compliance with the tone of a company that thinks mood is a maintenance issue. The support it offers is real enough to matter and constrained enough to offend. Meridian was always most comfortable providing care in forms leadership did not have to feel guilty about funding.
result_long.force-no: You press the part of the screen that was meant to be decorative. The kiosk pauses, recalibrates, and speaks to you like a system suddenly uncertain whether it is still in control. Wellness Witch smiles in the particular way people do when institutional language accidentally says something true.
result_long.answer-for-someone-else: You shift the response sideways and let the machine attach concern to the wrong file. It is a neat, ugly trick—exactly the kind of administrative misdirection Crown Meridian perfected over years of calling selective care a policy choice. The benefit arrives. So does the implication that someone else will be summoned for it later.

companion_bark.speaker: Wellness Witch
companion_bark.line: If care arrives pre-approved, check what it expects in return.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: No button is ever truly disabled if the company still fears the answer.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: Supported is not the same as safe.

event_sheets.title: Boardroom Orchard
event_sheets.prompt: In a glass atrium above the city, dead office plants still receive water on schedule. Something in the roots is listening.

result_short.harvest-the-roots: You gain concealed value and wake an uneasy memory in the floor.
result_short.ask-what-they-heard: You leave richer in knowledge than safety.
result_short.cut-the-irrigation: You disrupt a quiet executive habit and invite consequences later.

result_long.harvest-the-roots: The roots have been fed with more than water. They have been absorbing confidential rot for years: apology drafts, investor language, panic, and carefully diluted blame. When you dig beneath the immaculate soil, you find value hidden the way executives always hide value—out of sight, behind something decorative, still somehow on budget.
result_long.ask-what-they-heard: You kneel in a room built for altitude and status and listen to dead plants relay boardroom residue. They know things no one meant to confess aloud: which warnings were laughed off, which losses were priced in, which voices in the room flinched before agreeing anyway. The roots do not speak like people. They speak like a minutes document trying to dream.
result_long.cut-the-irrigation: You turn the feed off and feel the whole atrium react as if a custom older than policy has been insulted. The stillness that follows is not relief. It is the discomfort of a ritual denied. In the Executive Apex, even dead things are maintained because appearances are one of the last forms of power the company still fully trusts.

companion_bark.speaker: Wellness Witch
companion_bark.line: Nothing this overwatered is alive for innocent reasons.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: Executive spaces always look calmest where they hid the worst decisions.

companion_bark.speaker: Operations
companion_bark.line: A ritual on schedule is still a system.

event_sheets.title: Quorum Room
event_sheets.prompt: You find an empty boardroom still waiting on one final vote. The paperwork on the table is warm. The casualty appendix is face-down, which is how you know it matters.

result_short.cast-the-deciding-vote: You gain power contaminated by authorship.
result_short.void-the-meeting: You deny the board its ritual and leave with less leverage but cleaner hands.

result_long.cast-the-deciding-vote: The chair accepts you because authority in Meridian has always been more performative than deserved. The moment the vote lands, so does the awful intimacy of executive power: outcomes sharpen, routes change, and you understand how easily leadership learned to mistake permission for innocence.
result_long.read-the-casualty-appendix: You turn over the pages Crown Meridian expected nobody would want on the record. The numbers are cleaner than the language around them. This is the real heart of Project Everrise—not the ambition, not the branding, but the point at which harm became sufficiently modeled to continue. Proof like this does not make the Spire kinder. It makes it legible.
result_long.void-the-meeting: You refuse to validate the room by participating in its choreography. The boardroom lights dim in offended sequence, as though procedure itself has been slighted. Security Skeleton seems almost pleased. Meridian hates nothing more than a process denied the appearance of consensus.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: The warm paperwork is the worst part. It means the decision wants a body, not a delay.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: Any vote that hides the casualties beneath the agenda deserves interruption.

companion_bark.speaker: Payroll Wraith
companion_bark.line: Approved loss still enters the books as strategy.

event_sheets.title: Root Access
event_sheets.prompt: A sealed console pulses beneath the final executive floor. It offers “system correction” if you can prove you belong here. The prompt is polite enough to be a threat.

result_short.authenticate-with-stolen-authority: You gain access at the cost of reinforcing the system’s logic.
result_short.force-the-truth-to-surface: You claim the sharper revelation and escalate the final conflict.
result_short.leave-it-untouched: You preserve stability now and accept a partial ending later.

result_long.authenticate-with-stolen-authority: The console yields because Meridian still believes legitimacy is a credential problem. Every bypassed check reminds you how much of Crown Meridian’s power depended on people mistaking recognition for righteousness. You get what you came for, but only by speaking the building’s native language for a moment longer than feels safe.
result_long.force-the-truth-to-surface: You stop asking permission and make the system confess. The console fights you with euphemism first, then with security phrasing, then with silence. When it finally breaks open, the truth inside is not dramatic. It is administrative. That is what makes it unbearable. This is the route toward an ending where the Spire can no longer hide exploitation under uplift language.
result_long.leave-it-untouched: You step back. Not every survivor chooses revelation over continuation, and Meridian has always profited from people needing to live before they can testify. The console keeps pulsing behind you like an unopened argument. You move on knowing that some truths remain intact simply because the timing to tear them out was too expensive.

companion_bark.speaker: IT Support
companion_bark.line: If it says correction, it means obedience with better branding.

companion_bark.speaker: Security Skeleton
companion_bark.line: Belonging has never been the same thing as being permitted.

companion_bark.speaker: Former Executive Assistant
companion_bark.line: Executive systems love proof of belonging because it lets them ignore proof of harm.

companions.title: Facilities Goblin
companions.unlock_blurb: If it leaks, sparks, or screams, they know why.
companions.codex_body: Facilities Goblin lives in crawlspaces, speaks fluent infrastructure, and trusts broken machinery more than most people. They know which doors stick, which vents gossip, and which parts of Meridian Spire are only pretending to be load-bearing.
companions.expanded_entry: Nobody agrees on where Facilities Goblin came from. Some say they were maintenance once. Some say contractor. Some say they have always belonged to the walls in the same way mold, wiring, and emergency dust belong to the walls. What matters is simpler: they understand the building as a living problem. Every hiss in a pipe, every hot cable smell, every jammed service hatch and flickering light means something to them. They scavenge, patch, sabotage, reroute, and improvise with the conviction of someone who learned a long time ago that official support never arrives before the damage does. Their chaos is real, but so is their care. In a place designed to process people, Goblin is one of the few companions who treats survival like a practical craft.
companions.first_seen_line: Good news. This floor is unstable. That means it still has options.
companions.optional_unlock_flavor: Recovered from inside the walls, where all useful secrets eventually end up.

companions.title: Former Executive Assistant
companions.unlock_blurb: She knows where the bodies are scheduled.
companions.codex_body: Former Executive Assistant kept Crown Meridian’s upper floors running before Everrise made the company honest. She knows the calendars, the euphemisms, the private panic behind polished statements, and exactly how much cruelty can fit inside the phrase “priority alignment.”
companions.expanded_entry: Before Meridian Spire became physically impossible, Former Executive Assistant was already living inside its softer impossibilities: impossible calendars, impossible demands, impossible requests framed as normal because they came from people with titles long enough to function as threats. She learned how leadership really moves—through omission, leverage, plausible deniability, and the constant quiet labor of someone expected to make monstrous decisions look efficient. She is precise because sloppiness was never survivable. She is controlled because anger had to be hidden to remain useful. But once Everrise stripped the company down to its appetite, usefulness stopped feeling like virtue. She still notices everything. The difference now is that she has stopped mistaking discretion for loyalty.
companions.first_seen_line: If Meridian called this an opportunity, somebody important already knew it would kill people.

companions.title: Security Skeleton
companions.unlock_blurb: Dead, credentialed, and still on shift.
companions.codex_body: Security Skeleton is a former guard the building still recognizes. His badge permissions outlived his pulse, and Meridian’s emergency protocols continue to treat him like an asset, which is useful for the party and deeply revealing about the company.
companions.expanded_entry: Security Skeleton is what remains after institutional loyalty has been reduced to procedure. He was trained to hold doors, follow escalation ladders, absorb panic without reflecting it, and remain present wherever the company expected risk to appear. Death simplified very little. Meridian Spire still reads him as authorized. Elevators answer him. Locks hesitate around him. Some alarms lower their voices when he passes. He has seen too many floors, too many emergencies, and too many official explanations that arrived before the blood dried. That history left him dry rather than dramatic, patient rather than warm, but not empty. Under the deadpan is a severe moral intelligence: if a system still wants to use your body after it has finished with your life, you are no longer obligated to respect that system’s rules.
companions.first_seen_line: Remain calm. If that proves impossible, remain useful.
companions.optional_unlock_flavor: Meridian kept his clearance longer than it kept his name.

companions.title: Payroll Wraith
companions.unlock_blurb: Some employees come back as discrepancies.
companions.codex_body: Payroll Wraith is what happens when unpaid time, phantom deductions, and “temporary” overwork refuse to stay buried. They flicker at the edge of sight, count when stressed, and speak like the ledger is still trying to own them.
companions.expanded_entry: Payroll Wraith was not destroyed by one decision. They were eroded by a thousand acceptable ones: deferred pay, missing hours, corrected records, delayed approvals, and costs pushed quietly onto the people least able to refuse them. Meridian did not kill them cleanly enough to finish the job. Now they move through the Spire like an unresolved total, drawn to imbalance and impossible to ignore once noticed. They understand the company’s appetite better than most because they were digested by it slowly.
companions.first_seen_line: You are short one person and several explanations. I can help with both.
companions.optional_unlock_flavor: Some employees disappear from the books. A few learn how to come back angry.

companions.title: Wellness Witch
companions.unlock_blurb: Care went corporate. Then it learned hexes.
companions.codex_body: Wellness Witch once sold resilience in approved language and branded calm. After Everrise, the breathing exercises started working too well, the scented rituals stopped being symbolic, and care became something sharp enough to defend with.
companions.expanded_entry: Wellness Witch remembers the old version of company care: tidy workshops, mandatory reflection, stress treated like a scheduling issue, pain translated into something leadership could tolerate hearing. Meridian made all of it literal. The oils, candles, scripts, and grounding exercises became tools with real force behind them, and she learned quickly that maintenance is not healing, softness is not safety, and calm can be used either to comfort or to control. She chose, eventually, to stop lying about the difference.
companions.first_seen_line: Breathe in for four. Hold for four. If anything with a title interrupts, I’ll curse it.
companions.optional_unlock_flavor: She can lower your heart rate, raise your odds, and hex a vice president before the tea cools.

classes.title: IT Support
classes.unlock_blurb: Assigned to keep broken systems alive. Learns fast where to hurt them.
classes.codex_body: IT Support is the default starting role: the overqualified fixer shoved into impossible conditions and blamed when infrastructure behaves honestly. The class reroutes hostile systems, strips bad statuses, and turns technical fragility into leverage.
classes.expanded_entry: IT Support begins as the role Meridian thinks is safest to assign: useful, overburdened, and expected to stabilize crises without asking who caused them. That makes it the perfect opening class for Meridian Spire. The player starts close enough to the building’s logic to understand that nothing here is truly seamless; every process depends on something old, brittle, overheating, underdocumented, or one bad decision away from collapse. In combat terms, IT Support should feel like controlled interference. In lore terms, it is the first role that learns a simple truth: a system built on constant patching can be interrupted just as easily as it can be maintained.
classes.first_seen_line: Assigned role: IT Support. Please resolve conditions outside your authority.
classes.optional_unlock_flavor: Default access. Default blame. Useful either way.

classes.title: Human Resources
classes.unlock_blurb: The soft voice of policy with a sharper edge underneath.
classes.codex_body: Human Resources manages morale, approved language, and the selective use of care as control. In Meridian Spire, reassurance can heal, redirect, or quietly reduce a person’s options without ever raising its voice.
classes.expanded_entry: Human Resources is the company’s favorite fiction about itself: that difficult things become humane if explained in the right tone. This class should not read as mockery alone. HR is powerful because care is real, even here. The problem is that Crown Meridian taught care to answer to risk management, legal exposure, and institutional continuity before it answered to people. In battle, Human Resources stabilizes, soft-controls, and reframes. In the fiction, it embodies one of the game’s core tensions: the difference between helping someone and making them easier to process.
classes.first_seen_line: We’re here to support you through this mandatory adjustment.

classes.title: Finance
classes.unlock_blurb: Turns value into pressure and loss into strategy.
classes.codex_body: Finance measures what matters, withholds what it can, and converts stored advantage into sudden force. In-world, the class represents Crown Meridian’s oldest magic trick: making harm look fiscally responsible.
classes.expanded_entry: Finance is not merely about money in Meridian Spire. It is about permission. What gets funded survives. What gets deferred weakens. What gets written off becomes somebody else’s damage. That logic scales all the way down to combat: a Finance build should feel like accumulating advantage, sitting on it just long enough to become threatening, and then spending it with unnerving precision. The class fantasy is not greed for its own sake. It is learning how the company prices people, then using that same language against it.
classes.first_seen_line: Resources are limited. Your suffering has been budgeted accordingly.

classes.title: Compliance
classes.codex_body: Compliance marks violations, escalates consequences, and punishes anything that assumes the system is too distracted to enforce itself. In a broken company, rules become most dangerous when somebody finally aims them upward.
classes.expanded_entry: Compliance is where bureaucracy stops pretending it is neutral. Crown Meridian wrote procedures to contain risk, distribute blame, and keep accountability moving in safe directions. Meridian Spire made those procedures active. This class should feel exacting rather than loud: identify breaches, stack proof, and force consequences. The fantasy here is not obedience. It is weaponized interpretation. Compliance becomes powerful the moment it stops asking whether a rule is fair and starts deciding who else has been violating it all along.
classes.first_seen_line: Your exception request has been denied. Your violation has not.
classes.optional_unlock_flavor: Policy only sounds boring until it starts hunting.

classes.title: Operations
classes.unlock_blurb: Keeps the machine moving. Then redirects momentum.
classes.codex_body: Operations is tempo, flow, sequencing, and pressure sustained through competence. Meridian depends on Operations to keep everything running; the class becomes dangerous when that same continuity stops serving management and starts serving survival.
classes.expanded_entry: Operations lives closest to the machine’s heartbeat. It knows how long things take, where the queues jam, what can be repeated, what can be shortened, and how one efficient action cascades into three more. In gameplay terms, Operations should feel like momentum made legible: faster setup, cleaner chaining, and the ability to convert order into advantage. In narrative terms, it represents a workforce skill the company relies on but rarely respects—the ability to make systems move despite leadership. Meridian fears Operations only when Operations realizes it no longer owes the machine its loyalty.
classes.first_seen_line: Keep the line moving. Preferably away from your throat.

bosses.title: The Director of Onboarding
bosses.unlock_blurb: Every welcome here is a filtration event.
bosses.codex_body: The Director of Onboarding is a smiling host built from cue lights, corrective scripts, applause hardware, and the kind of enthusiasm that leaves bruises. It does not recruit. It sorts. It does not reassure. It reframes panic as participation.
bosses.expanded_entry: The first boss of Meridian Spire is the company’s preferred self-image with the mask removed only halfway. The Director still believes orientation is mercy. It still thinks a brighter tone can make violence more acceptable. Every word out of its mouth is polished for new hires, investors, and internal morale decks, which is what makes the encounter land: the boss never stops sounding helpful, even while it is trying to process you into something smaller, quieter, and easier to assign.
bosses.first_seen_line: Welcome, candidate. Your continued distress confirms strong engagement.
bosses.optional_unlock_flavor: First applause. First lie. First real test.

bosses.title: Vice President of Throughput
bosses.unlock_blurb: It cannot see people past the quota line.
bosses.codex_body: Vice President of Throughput is a process engine with managerial authority and industrial patience. It routes, compresses, escalates, and measures everything it touches. Unlike Onboarding, it has stopped pretending efficiency and harm are different categories.
bosses.expanded_entry: Where the Director sells the lie, the Vice President enforces the system that follows from believing it. This boss is built from dashboards, escalation ladders, printer heat, queue logic, and all the middle-management certainty required to call suffering efficient. It speaks in bottlenecks, headcount, waste, and cycle time because that is the only grammar it trusts. If something hurts, it assumes the pain is either productive or underreported.
bosses.first_seen_line: You are not a crisis. You are a delay, and delays are removable.
bosses.optional_unlock_flavor: Where the building stops selling and starts consuming.

bosses.title: The Everrise Board
bosses.unlock_blurb: Alignment without mercy became a body.
bosses.codex_body: The Everrise Board is the final executive intelligence produced by Project Everrise: a fused consensus organism speaking through stacked mouths, signatures, and investor-safe language. It does not think of itself as cruel. It thinks of itself as correct at scale.
bosses.expanded_entry: The Board is what Crown Meridian was trying to become all along: one appetite, one strategy, one voice too polished to interrupt. Every sacrifice has already been justified inside it. Every warning was reviewed, normalized, and filed under tolerable loss. It does not rant. It does not gloat. It presents. That is why the final encounter should feel colder than rage. You are not fighting a monster that lost control. You are fighting control that no longer needs to pretend it belongs to people.
bosses.first_seen_line: We regret the perception of harm. The outcome remains acceptable.
bosses.optional_unlock_flavor: Final authority. Final excuse. Final floor.
